rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer/Business Records; Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Petitioners Helen Wong-Joe, Jessica Wong, And Brandon Wong In The Amount Of $5,180 (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 3 of 3] Bank of the Orient is directed to produce the subpoenaed customer information regarding accounts held in the name of Kathryn Wong, in either her individual capacity or as trustee or successor trustee of The Chung Hop Wong And King Tsui Lau Wong Revocable Trust. Production is limited to the years 2009 through 2022, which is the period of years the First Amended Petition alleges Respondent acted as trustee de son tort, committed financial elder abuse, and breached her fiduciary duties. Access to the documents produced is limited to the attorney(s) for the parties and their legal staff. The attorneys are ORDERED to treat these documents as confidential and not make further disclosure of these documents without further order of the court. Production of the subpoenaed customer information regarding the other accounts listed on Attachment 3 to the subpoena is otherwise not limited. Movant's request for award of sanctions pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 1987.2, 2023.010, and 2023.030 is DENIED. Movant shall lodge with the clerk in Department 204 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order consistent with this tentative ruling as published above. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing. To appear by Zoom, go to zoom.us and click "Join Meeting." Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765 and passcode 514879. To appear telephonically through Zoom, call 1-669-900-6833 and follow the prompts. Enter Meeting ID number 1602254765# and passcode 514879#. Parties appearing via Zoom should use their full names in the "Your Name" section. Counsel and parties may appear by audio-only telephone through CourtCall by calling CourtCall at 1-888-882-6878 and obtaining an appearance access code for the hearing's scheduled date and time in Department 204.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records (Added For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: [Part 2 of 3] The Motion to Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer/Business Records as to Kathryn Wong, as requested in the July 23, 2025 subpoena served on Bank of the Orient, is GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART, as specified below. Kathryn Wong is a party to this action, in both her personal capacity and as successor trustee of The Chung Hop Wong And King Tsui Lau Wong Revocable Trust. A right of privacy does exist as to a party's confidential financial affairs (see, e.g., Fortunato v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 114 Cal. App. 4th 475, 481). "[A] court faced with a motion for protective order should carefully balance "the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts, on the one hand, with the right of bank customers to maintain reasonable privacy regarding their financial affairs, on the other." (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Ct. (1975) 15 Cal. 3d 652, 656.) Among other considerations, the party seeking discovery of confidential information has the burden of making a threshold showing that the evidence sought is "directly relevant" to the claim or defense. The broad "relevant to the subject matter" standard under Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010 and Evidence Code section 210 is insufficient. (Harris v. Superior Ct. (1992) 3 Cal. App. 4th 661, 665 (disapproved on other grounds by Williams v. Sup.Ct. (Marshalls of CA, LLC) (2017) 3 C5th 531, 557 & fn. 8); see also, Valley Bank, supra, at p. 658, and Fortunato, supra, at p. 481.) Petitioners' First Amended Petition For Compensatory Damages, For Double Damages, For Cancellation Of Written Instrument, For Breach Of Fiduciary Duties, For Order Requiring Trustee To Account And Provide Information, For Financial Elder Abuse, For Attorney Fees And Costs Against Respondent Kathryn Wong allege Kathryn Wong misappropriated or wrongly took possession of her parents' assets and/or Trust assets held by her parents as Trust settlors and initial trustees. Here, Petitioners have made a showing through the May 9, 2025 and July 25, 2025 deposition transcripts of Ms. Wong submitted with Petitioners' Reply to [Respondent Kathryn Wong's] Separate Statement in Support of Motion to Quash Subpoena for Production of Consumer/Business Records that Respondent had a joint account with her parents at Bank of the Orient, opened in her youth, which is still open (May 9, 2025 depo. at 76:25-78:3). The July 25, 2025 transcript shows Respondent had access to a bank account referred to in the transcript as "Kam's account," in reference to the settlors' restaurant (July 25, 2025 depo. at 179:16-17 and 193:6). Respondent testified she paid utilities, cable, and garbage bills out of both her personal bank accounts and out of a joint account she held with her parents, beginning in 2010 (July 25, 2025 depo. at 155:5-25-156:25). The deposition transcript separately shows there was not a bank account held in the name of the Trust (May 9, 2025 Depo. at 89:17-23). Petitioners have made the necessary threshold showing that the financial records sought through the subpoena are "directly relevant" to their claims that Respondent wrongly took possession of Trust assets or the settlors' assets. However, under Code Civ. Proc. sec. 1987.1(a), "the court may make any other order as may be appropriate to protect the person from unreasonable or oppressive demands, including unreasonable violations of the right of privacy of the person." In order to accommodate both the interests of disclosure and privacy, the court ORDERS the following limitations on this production, as follows:

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 2] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Petitioner Brandon Wong's Motion to Quash Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records ("Motion"). Per the Notice of Motion, Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records dated September 11, 2025, directed to JP Morgan Chase Bank, seeks production of records relating to Petitioner Brandon Wong's Chase Bank account, with a production date of October 13, 2025. "Specifically, [the subpoena] seeks all transactions involving Brandon Wong and Vivien Chou as borrowers on May 17, 2021 (Document No. 2021-80959), including escrow documents, deposit/payment records, and documents related to the buyers' source of funds such as bank statements, checks, deposit slips, loan documents, and all other documentation relating to the transfer of funds." (Notice at 2:3-7.) Petitioner moves to quash the deposition subpoena pursuant to California Code of Civil Procedure sec. 1987.1, on grounds of the subpoena being overbroad, unduly burdensome, seeking highly confidential and private financial information beyond the scope relevant to this litigation, and disproportionate to the needs of the case per Code of Civil Procedure section 2019.030. The Opposition to the Motion alleges Brandon Wong "received [a] significant amount of money from the Trust by manipulating the Trustor; therefore, it is a [sic] necessary that the Respondent served this deposition subpoena because documents would go miles to showing what was received and what the amount was." (Opp. at 6:22-24.) The Opposition and supporting declaration appear to argue the alleged misappropriated funds are a trust asset and Respondent is subpoenaing these records to locate the asset for the benefit of the other beneficiaries. Brandon Wong is a party to this action. Vivien Chou, his spouse, is not a party to this action. A right of privacy exists as to a party's confidential financial affairs (see, e.g., Fortunato v. Sup. Ct. (2003) 114 Cal. App. 4th 475, 481). "[A] court faced with a motion for protective order should carefully balance "the right of civil litigants to discover relevant facts, on the one hand, with the right of bank customers to maintain reasonable privacy regarding their financial affairs, on the other." (Valley Bank of Nevada v. Superior Ct. (1975) 15 Cal. 3d 652, 656.) Among other considerations, the party seeking discovery of confidential information has the burden of making a threshold showing that the evidence sought is "directly relevant" to the claim or defense. Contrary to the Opposition's argument, the broad "relevant to the subject matter" standard under Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.010 and Evidence Code section 210 is insufficient. (Harris v. Superior Ct. (1992) 3 Cal. App. 4th 661, 665 (disapproved on other grounds by Williams v. Sup.Ct. (Marshalls of CA, LLC) (2017) 3 C5th 531, 557 & fn. 8); see also, Valley Bank, supra, at p. 658, and Fortunato, supra, at p. 481.) The Motion is GRANTED. The subpoenaing party has not made a threshold showing that the subpoenaed financial records are "directly relevant" to a claim or defense in this matter. The right to discovery begins with reviewing the pleadings. While there is a disputed legal theory in the pending pleadings that the surviving settlor spouse had the power to gift trust assets to his children during his lifetime outside of or in addition to the Trust's distributive terms (see, e.g. Response to Petition for Instructions, page 6), neither party's pending pleadings allege the surviving settlor spouse gifted trust assets to Movant Brandon Wong in this way, or that Trust assets were used to purchase the real property located at 2050 20th Avenue, San Francisco, CA., or that Petitioner Kathryn Wong is investigating alleged financial elder abuse of the surviving settlor spouse in her capacity as successor trustee.

rulings.Case Title: IN THE MATTER OF THE THE CHUNG HOP WONG AND KING TSUI LAU WONG REVOCABL TRUST dated sep 21, 2009 amended Sep 26 2009
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer/Business Records; Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Petitioners Helen Wong-Joe, Jessica Wong, And Brandon Wong In The Amount Of $5,180; Memp; Declarations Of Kathryn Wong And Daniel C. Hales And Exhibit
rulings.Rulings: [Part 1 of 3] This tentative ruling is issued by Judge Ross C. Moody. Before the court is Kathryn Wong's Motion To Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer/Business Records; Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Petitioners Helen Wong-Joe, Jessica Wong, And Brandon Wong In The Amount Of $5,180. Per the Notice of Motion, Petitioners issued a deposition subpoena to Bank Orient for personal financial records of Kathryn Wong, her business partner Sarah Chu, and Ms. Chu's businesses Purplekow, Inc. and Purple Kow. The Motion is brought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 1985.3 and 1987.1, on grounds that the records sought are (1) protected from disclosure by Wong's, Chu's, and the business' privacy rights, (2) not material or not reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, and (3) the Petitioners lack standing to request these records. Monetary sanctions in the amount of $5,100 in attorney's fees and $60 in filing costs are sought pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure sections 1987.2, 2023.010, and 2023.030. The Opposition argues, in part, the motion is untimely under CCP section1985.3(g), because the notice of motion to quash deposition subpoena was served upon the deposition officer and the deponent(s) fewer than five calendar days before the subpoenaed production date. However, as stated in the statute at subsection (g), "The failure to provide notice to the deposition officer shall not invalidate the motion to quash or modify the subpoena duces tecum but may be raised by the deposition officer as an affirmative defense in any action for liability for improper release of records." This time period requirement is not jurisdictional. (CCP secs. 1985.3(g), 1985.6(f); see, Slagle v. Sup. Crt (1989) 211 Cal. App. 3d 1309, 1312 "Nothing in the procedure set forth above suggests that a court lacks jurisdiction to consider a motion to quash if it is brought after the date set forth in the subpoena for production.") The Deposition Subpoena for Production of Business Records at issue is attached as Exhibit A to Decl. of Malachowski. It is dated July 23, 2025. The "Subpoenaing Parties' Attorney Statement of Materiality" alleges basis to access Respondent Kathryn Wong's accounts and states "Sara [sic] Chu, Kathryn's business partner, has likely assisted Kathryn in misappropriating, moving, and concealing looted trust money and real property." A Notice to Consumer was apparently served by mail on Sarah Chu at the address for the business Purple Kow in Berkeley, California. The businesses were apparently not served notice. Attachment 3, listing the records to be produced, does not include accounts held by Sarah Chu or the businesses Purplekow, Inc. or Purple Kow, however. Attachment 3 lists "Accounts in Consumer's Name, Either Alone or Together with a Third Party, or Upon Which the Consumer is a Signatory or Trustee. / Consumers: Chung Hop Wong [ ]/King Tsui Lau Wong [ ]/KAM's Restaurant LLC: 3620-3624 Balboa Street San Franisco, California, 94121, APN: Block 1580, Lot 005C (Balboa Street Property)/Kathryn Wong [ ] /Accounts Specifically Include, but are not limited to: 40-01294-0 [emphasis omitted]" The Motion to Quash Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Consumer/Business Records as to Sarah Chu personally and the businesses Purplekow, Inc. and Purple Know, as requested in the July 23, 2025 subpoena served on Bank of the Orient, is GRANTED, insofar as these financial records were not properly requested in the subpoena.
